findings.finding: The managed Codex and Claude paths are compiled as read-only, so the core Fabric cannot yet serve as the general managed implementation plane.
findings.recommendation: Introduce provider-neutral authority profiles and compile them into native settings; retain the current posture as review-readonly and add workspace-write-offline first.
findings.acceptance: Both primaries pass a hermetic fixture that writes only inside an owned worktree, cannot access a sibling scope, has no network, cannot perform an external effect, and records effective settings.

findings.finding: The Fabric class is a roughly 7,400-line transaction façade combining policy, SQL, provider execution, recovery, budgets, barriers and projections.
findings.recommendation: Extract bounded command handlers behind one UnitOfWork while preserving one process, one SQLite authority and a temporary compatibility façade.
findings.acceptance: No application command handler directly depends on unrelated bounded contexts; architecture tests enforce dependencies; characterisation and recovery tests remain green.

findings.finding: Portable adapter policy and machine-local executable paths, digests, platform and local observations are mixed in tracked configuration.
findings.recommendation: Split portable adapter catalogue/policy from gitignored local attestations and project/user activation overlays.
findings.acceptance: A clean checkout contains no machine path or local executable digest; doctor produces a signed/hash-bound local attestation consumed through an explicit overlay.

findings.finding: Lifecycle, review and authority rules are repeated across HARNESS.md, skills, specifications, validators and runbooks.
findings.recommendation: Create a versioned executable lifecycle policy; keep the constitution concise and make skills supply methods rather than duplicate gates.
findings.acceptance: One policy version determines route, required gates, repair bounds and retention; generated docs and fixtures prove all surfaces agree.

findings.finding: The README says 34 skills while the canonical catalogue contains 33; the harness check counts skills but does not validate the README assertion.
findings.recommendation: Generate the README catalogue/count from a canonical manifest and fail CI on a dirty regeneration.
findings.acceptance: There is no manually maintained count; adding/removing a skill changes generated documentation and provider metadata in one check.

findings.finding: All five active specifications still report implementation, integrated verification, provider review or human acceptance as pending/in progress.
findings.recommendation: Publish a current conformance matrix linking each normative requirement to code, deterministic test, live smoke, independent review and human acceptance.
findings.acceptance: No specification is called complete until every required evidence cell is present and current for the released commit.

findings.finding: The security catalogue declares secret scanning, SAST, licence, provenance, prompt-injection and other checks, while the shipped static scanner is a narrow Python AST pattern check.
findings.recommendation: Represent check implementation status explicitly and wire real deterministic tools or project-provided evidence for each required check.
findings.acceptance: A receipt cannot claim a declared check unless an implementation/external evidence source, version, scope and result are recorded.

findings.finding: Retention is report/archive-only and deliberately has no apply/delete path, which prevents governed pruning of owned ephemeral and operational state.
findings.recommendation: Add retention classes, preview, legal hold, redaction, compact and typed delete with protected paths and receipts.
findings.acceptance: A test run can delete only known harness-owned ephemeral state, preserve evidence, honour holds and refuse unknown files.

findings.finding: There is no typed, human-approved backlog queue connecting scope, readiness, authority, expiry and execution.
findings.recommendation: Add backlog item schema and a queue controller with approval digest, dependencies, limits and terminal states.
findings.acceptance: The controller cannot claim a stale, unapproved, dependency-blocked or over-budget item and invalidates approval on scope changes.

findings.finding: The requested request-understanding/decomposition/model-selection behaviour is not represented as a small typed intake decision owned by the runtime.
findings.recommendation: Implement an intake decision kernel that classifies outcome, ambiguity, risk, authority, decomposition, evidence and lifecycle route.
findings.acceptance: Every run begins with a versioned intake decision; low-risk direct tasks remain lightweight; broad tasks show a preliminary topology and decision needs.

findings.finding: Four local Node packages use separate lockfiles and recursively install/build one another; there is no root workspace task graph.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a root npm or pnpm workspace, one lockfile, TypeScript project references and root check/test/audit commands before considering Nx/Turbo.
findings.acceptance: A clean root install builds packages in dependency order once; CI caches one lock and package checks cannot silently use stale local builds.

findings.finding: The Console has good model/controller separation, but index.ts still combines Unicode handling, layout, rendering and mouse interaction across roughly 1,800 lines.
findings.recommendation: Split text/graphemes, layout, render primitives, views, hit regions and mouse interaction; keep snapshots and controller interfaces stable.
findings.acceptance: View modules can be tested independently and the main entry point is a composition/export surface rather than a renderer implementation.

findings.finding: The Fabric package exports a broad set of daemon, Git, Herdr, operator, routing, profile and domain internals from its root.
findings.recommendation: Shrink the public surface to runtime factory/client/protocol types and explicit subpath exports for supported integration ports.
findings.acceptance: Internal modules are inaccessible through the package root; API-extractor or an export snapshot guards the supported surface.

findings.finding: Routing combines dated model aliases, regex family inference and limited runtime discovery; capability and outcome feedback are not a full closed loop.
findings.recommendation: Make aliases intent bands, prefer provider-native capability discovery, score candidates on quality/cost/latency/tools/privacy/availability and calibrate from receipts.
findings.acceptance: Every route receipt explains constraints, candidate scores, selection, substitution and later outcome; stale catalogues cannot silently decide.

findings.finding: The activation surface carries five active adapters and additional broker concepts while the two primary implementation paths remain incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Define a core distribution containing Claude and Codex; move extras to opt-in adapters with independent conformance and support levels.
findings.acceptance: Core install and CI do not require optional providers; optional failures cannot weaken or block the two-primary path.

findings.finding: Other-primary review is broadly mandatory for substantial work, which is simple but can be costly and insensitive to oracle strength.
findings.recommendation: Preserve paired scoping as the broad-task default but make certifying review risk-, novelty-, independence- and oracle-aware.
findings.acceptance: The lifecycle engine derives reviewers from risk controls; exceptions/degradation are explicit; deterministic oracles remain primary.

findings.finding: The scope skill's one-question-per-round rule can make broad collaboration unnecessarily serial.
findings.recommendation: Use small decision packets with recommended defaults; reserve one-at-a-time questioning for genuinely dependent choices.
findings.acceptance: Wide-scope fixtures reach an approved contract with fewer conversational turns without hiding consequential decisions.

findings.finding: The orchestration skill defaults to fan-out for bounded work without an explicit expected-information-gain/coordination-cost test.
findings.recommendation: Require a decomposition test: independent information, stable interfaces, non-overlapping writes and expected value greater than coordination cost.
findings.acceptance: Fixtures choose serial work for tightly coupled tasks and parallel work only where the return contract is independently checkable.

findings.finding: No permanent fix without a reproduction/root cause is a strong default but lacks typed emergency and irreproducible-incident exceptions.
findings.recommendation: Add containment, external outage, nondeterministic production-only and observation-only exception records; never label containment as root-cause closure.
findings.acceptance: Emergency mitigation can proceed under bounded authority with follow-up obligations and evidence of unresolved cause.

findings.finding: The seam-first default can encourage transitional compatibility layers even in a pre-release project without consumers.
findings.recommendation: Default to direct replacement before stable release; require consumer evidence, owner, expiry, telemetry and removal test for compatibility.
findings.acceptance: A policy check rejects new legacy/compat paths without a waiver ADR satisfying the required fields.

findings.finding: The TDD skill has some exceptions but does not define a uniform machine-readable exception record across delivery.
findings.recommendation: Add typed exceptions for generated output, declarative migrations, security containment, exploratory spikes and legacy seams.
findings.acceptance: Every skipped red test has a reason, scope, compensating evidence, owner and expiry/follow-up.

findings.finding: The autonomous-lab contract emphasises continuation until a human STOP, which risks unproductive re-enumeration or budget exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Add complete, paused-budget, paused-decision, blocked-external, failed-invariant and idle-reenumeration terminal states.
findings.acceptance: A run exits or pauses deterministically at its ceiling and can resume from a canonical checkpoint.

findings.finding: Normative specs carry extensive amendment history, increasing context load and making current requirements harder to identify.
findings.recommendation: Keep a current normative document; move decisions to ADRs and revision history to a changelog/archive.
findings.acceptance: An agent can load current requirements without reading superseded amendment prose; traceability remains available.

findings.finding: The installer is safe about ownership but writes absolute repository paths and depends on Bash/POSIX and symlinks.
findings.recommendation: Create a cross-platform installer CLI with user/project scope, copy/link modes, profiles, marked blocks, rollback and doctor/update/uninstall.
findings.acceptance: A copied/package installation survives moving or deleting the source checkout; link mode is explicit development mode.

findings.finding: The global AGENTS file requires reading the whole constitution for orchestration decisions and makes `$caveman` the universal default style.
findings.recommendation: Use a small intake/bootstrap contract, path-scoped instructions and ordinary style guidance; remove the global pseudo-skill default.
findings.acceptance: Routine tasks load minimal instructions; specialised rules are loaded by path/trigger; output style remains domain appropriate.

findings.finding: Provider lifecycle hooks are not generated from one canonical hook policy and are not integrated as a standard Fabric attestation/receipt surface.
findings.recommendation: Compile a hook policy to Claude and Codex forms for session start/end, authority attestation, command/effect observation and receipt validation.
findings.acceptance: Provider hook configurations are generated, versioned and tested; the system remains secure when a hook is unavailable or bypassed.

findings.finding: Private files and Unix sockets do not by themselves isolate arbitrary processes running under the same operating-system identity.
findings.recommendation: Publish explicit cooperative/adversarial-input/adversarial-process modes and require substrate isolation for the strongest mode.
findings.acceptance: Security claims name the attacker model; write-enabled profiles have corresponding filesystem/network/credential isolation tests.

findings.finding: Typed Git and stable actions are strong, but there is no single uniform staged-effect model covering all external mutations.
findings.recommendation: Standardise effect proposals, validation, minimum-credential executors, idempotency and reconciliation.
findings.acceptance: Models cannot hold general external-write credentials; every effect has a proposal and executor receipt.

findings.finding: The helper requires explicit flags for every create/remove even though the policy permits a human-approved envelope; the runtime cannot yet prove the envelope automatically.
findings.recommendation: Let Fabric mint a short-lived worktree capability from the approved plan and pass an unforgeable reference rather than a self-attested flag.
findings.acceptance: The helper verifies the exact run/task/repo/operation capability; a copied flag without a capability is rejected.

findings.finding: Budgets are recorded, but route selection and topology do not yet learn systematically from cost, latency, review yield and outcome.
findings.recommendation: Add predicted and actual utility measures; calibrate routes and agent-team templates from held-out outcomes.
findings.acceptance: Retrospectives can compare predicted/actual cost, duration, defects and reviewer yield by route without exposing private content.

findings.finding: Events and projections exist, but operator replay/time-travel is not a first-class Console capability.
findings.recommendation: Add cursor-based replay, immutable projection snapshots and exportable incident timelines.
findings.acceptance: An operator can reconstruct why a gate, route or effect changed at a prior sequence and verify source revisions.

findings.finding: Fabric tracks provider sessions, but there is no complete provider-neutral contract for projecting task/model/effort/topology into native Codex and Claude UIs.
findings.recommendation: Add a native presentation adapter that sets supported names/goals/metadata and emits concise start/status/final summaries with Fabric IDs.
findings.acceptance: Users can identify task, role, model/effort and parent/child relation in native UIs without reading the Fabric database.

findings.finding: Strict TypeScript settings are strong, but no explicit dependency-boundary test was identified for runtime contexts.
findings.recommendation: Add import-boundary rules or architecture tests before extraction to stop new cycles and Console/runtime leakage.
findings.acceptance: CI rejects forbidden context imports and broad root-internal imports.

findings.finding: Package scripts include typecheck/build/test but no visible lint, format check or coverage threshold.
findings.recommendation: Add a low-noise formatter/linter and risk-weighted coverage/contract thresholds rather than chasing one global percentage.
findings.acceptance: Changed critical policy and recovery paths require branch/condition coverage or explicit waiver; formatting is deterministic.

findings.finding: CI is Ubuntu-only while production configuration and IPC/file-mode behaviour are macOS/POSIX specific.
findings.recommendation: Either declare supported macOS/Linux targets and test both, or abstract IPC and permissions before claiming Windows support.
findings.acceptance: The support matrix is explicit and every supported OS runs installer, database, IPC and Console smoke tests.

findings.finding: The review could not verify current-head checks or branch protection from available connector evidence, despite a substantive CI workflow.
findings.recommendation: Require named checks on protected main, forbid direct unverified pushes for release candidates and show check state in the operator projection.
findings.acceptance: The released commit has independently verifiable required checks and a branch/ruleset configuration test or documented verification.

findings.finding: CODEOWNERS assigns the repository to a single individual, so code-owner approval cannot provide independent human review.
findings.recommendation: Keep single ownership if this is a personal project, but do not describe CODEOWNERS as independence; add teams/collaborators when human independence is required.
findings.acceptance: Governance documentation distinguishes model review, maintainer ownership and independent human approval.

findings.finding: No CONTRIBUTING.md was found at the baseline, despite public visibility and substantial maintenance rules.
findings.recommendation: Add a concise contribution guide or explicitly state that external contributions are not currently accepted.
findings.acceptance: A contributor can identify setup, tests, supported platforms, design authority and review expectations.

findings.finding: There is no governed notes inbox with promotion/expiry, while the user intends agents to retain random decisions and ideas.
findings.recommendation: Add a project notes inbox with owner, created date, expiry and promotion target; never treat notes as canonical truth.
findings.acceptance: A maintenance check reports expired notes and validates promotion links.

findings.finding: Dependency audit and action pinning are strong, but no release SBOM, provenance attestation or signed release workflow was identified.
findings.recommendation: Add reproducible release artefacts, SBOM, provenance and signing when a distributable package/binary is published.
findings.acceptance: Users can verify source commit, dependencies, build workflow and artefact digest.

findings.finding: Normal tests intentionally use fakes and do not prove live login, provider calls or human Console identification.
findings.recommendation: Keep hermetic CI, but add an explicitly authorised release-candidate acceptance suite with bounded real-provider calls and human usability evidence.
findings.acceptance: Live evidence is dated, version-bound and separate from deterministic CI; absence cannot be misrepresented as proof.

findings.finding: Exact executable versions and digests improve assurance but create frequent maintenance churn and can conflate compatibility with one workstation.
findings.recommendation: Keep exact local attestations; define portable tested ranges/capabilities separately and require conformance for an unseen compatible version.
findings.acceptance: A new provider version can be assessed without editing portable machine paths, while untested versions still fail or degrade explicitly.

findings.finding: The control plane is tied to Unix sockets and POSIX identity/file-mode concepts.
findings.recommendation: Abstract local transport and credential storage behind ports; retain Unix sockets as the current POSIX implementation.
findings.acceptance: Protocol clients are transport-neutral and platform support is decided by tested transport implementations.

findings.finding: Forcing every description to start with 'Use' and include an exclusion in the first 250 characters improves routing discipline but makes semantic metadata depend on prose shape.
findings.recommendation: Move triggers, exclusions, authority and artefact class into a canonical manifest while retaining human-readable descriptions.
findings.acceptance: Routing fixtures use structured metadata and descriptions can optimise clarity without losing machine checks.

findings.finding: Status, doctor, installer planning, model routing, worktrees and retention are separate commands without a unified product CLI.
findings.recommendation: Expose a single `harness` CLI with stable subcommands and machine-readable output; preserve specialised binaries internally.
findings.acceptance: `harness doctor` reports installation, provider compatibility, project trust, Fabric, Console, hooks, policies and current-head evidence.

findings.finding: Retrospective and evaluation primitives exist, but there is no governed runtime loop that converts observations into falsifiable harness proposals.
findings.recommendation: Add proposal-first self-improvement: predict impact, run held-out evaluation, require approval, deploy, observe and roll back.
findings.acceptance: The harness cannot silently edit itself; every accepted change has a prediction, evaluation lineage and post-change result.
